title: Data-Driven Modelling to predict forest fire spread in the Patagonian region in Argentina
authors: Lucas Becerra, Monica Malen Denham, Alejandro B. Kolton, Karina Laneri
abstract: Wildfires are among the most severe disturbances affecting forest ecosystems, with over 50,000 hectares burned in Patagonia, Argentina, during 2025 alone. This study implements a Reaction-Diffusion-Convection (RDC) model to simulate wildfire spread in the Steffen and Martin Lakes area, a region severely impacted by fires. By integrating high-resolution maps of slope, wind velocity, and vegetation, we conducted three computational experiments of increasing complexity to simulate fire propagation across heterogeneous landscapes.   We employed a Genetic Algorithm (GA) to recover reference model parameters by maximizing the spatial overlap between simulated and reference burned areas. Subsequently, parameter estimates were refined using XGBoost to improve accuracy. Results demonstrate that the GA accurately recovers reference parameters across all scenarios, while the XGBoost fine-tuning significantly enhances accuracy in simpler cases. This integrated framework offers a systematic approach for estimating difficult-to-measure wildfire parameters, demonstrating the potential of hybrid computational methods for wildfire modeling and forest management.
summary: This paper presents a hybrid computational method that uses machine learning to optimize a physics-based reaction-diffusion-convection model for predicting forest fires. By combining Genetic Algorithms with XGBoost, the researchers can efficiently estimate difficult-to-measure environmental parameters in complex, heterogeneous landscapes.
main_problem: The difficulty of accurately predicting wildfire spread in Patagonia due to the lack of local parameters and the inadequacy of global models for heterogeneous landscapes.
main_result: A hybrid framework combining a Genetic Algorithm with XGBoost successfully recovers model parameters, with XGBoost significantly improving accuracy in low-dimensional scenarios.
method: A hybrid approach using a Genetic Algorithm (GA) for global parameter search and an XGBoost surrogate model for fine-tuning and accelerating the optimization process.
model_or_system: A Reaction-Diffusion-Convection (RDC) model, phenomenologically inspired by the SIR epidemic framework, applied to wildfire propagation across a heterogeneous landscape of different vegetation types.
key_observables: Spatial overlap between simulated and reference burned areas, fire front velocity, and fire front width.
important_parameters: Ignition rate (beta), extinction rate (gamma), diffusion constant (D), advection weights (A and B for wind and slope), and ignition coordinates (x, y).
main_assumptions: The diffusion constant is assumed to be homogeneous; the wind field is assumed to be stationary; the model does not capture microscopic combustion physics.
figures_summary: Figure 1-3 show reference wildfire patterns for different experimental complexities; Figure 4 shows fitness evolution; Figure 5 shows a fitness valley in the beta-gamma landscape; Figure 6 shows parameter correlation; Figure 7 shows convergence of ignition coordinates; Figure 8 shows spatial difference maps.
paper_structure: Introduction to the wildfire problem, description of the RDC model and numerical implementation, presentation of the GA and XGBoost optimization framework, results from three experiments of increasing complexity, and discussion of limitations and future work.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Reference wildfire for the first experiment, simulated with D = 10 m2 h−1, A = 1 × 10−4, B = 15 m h−1, and ignition point at (x, y) = (400, 600). Each green area corresponds to a different fuel type (Forest A, Forest B, exotic forest, pasture, and shrubland), with spatially heterogeneous β and γ values listed in Table 1. Red indicates the final burned area (final state after 500 steps, dt = 0.5 h), yielding a burnt area of approximately 1015 ha; the star marks the ignition point; arrows indicate wind direction.

Figure 2: Reference wildfire for the second experiment (Steffen-Martin landscape). The same reference values as the first experiment were used for (D = 10 m2 h−1, A = 1×10−4, B = 15 m h−1, and ignition point at (x, y) = (400, 600)), while homogeneous β and γ were introduced as additional tunable parameters with reference values (β = 1.5 h−1

Figure 3: Reference wildfire used in Experiment 3 (Steffen-Martin landscape). The simula- tion uses D = 10 m2 h−1, A = 1×10−4, and B = 15 m h−1, with three fixed ignition points at (1130, 290), (1300, 150), and (620, 280). Each green area corresponds to a different fuel type (Forest A, Forest B, exotic forest, pasture, and shrubland), with heterogeneous and tunable βi and γi values (Table 1). Red indicates the final burned area (final state after 500 steps, dt = 0.5 h), yielding a burnt area of approximately 4241 ha; this reference fire was designed to burn a representative fraction of all fuel types.

Figure 4: Evolution of the fitness in the three experiments. The dashed line shows the mean fitness per generation, while the solid line indicates the minimum fitness (best individual). Elitism ensures that the best individual is preserved across generations.

Figure 5: Fitness landscape in relation to β and γ for the second case. It is shown that there is a valley of similar fitness values, which creates a strong correlation between both parameters and hinders their accurate estimation. The red point indicates the reference values (β, γ) = (1.5, 0.5).
